title: Chapter 1: Game Over
content: The pounding on his door sounded exactly like a boss fight rhythm pattern.

Marcus Chen paused his MMO raid, fingers frozen above the keyboard. Three sharp impacts. A pause. Then three more. Outside, something groaned—not the squeaky-hinge groan of Mrs. Patterson from 4A, but a wet, throat-full-of-gravel sound that made his spine lock.
He pulled off his headset.
The groaning continued. Not at his door anymore—down the hall. A crash. Glass breaking. Someone screaming, the sound cutting off like a guillotine drop.

Marcus's hands moved on autopilot. Deadbolt. Chain. The little wedge he'd bought from a late-night infomercial at 3 AM because the announcer had seemed so convinced. He pressed his back against the door.

Through the peephole: Mrs. Patterson. Except Mrs. Patterson's throat was open, dark fluid pulsing down her housecoat, and her eyes were the cloudy grey of a dead monitor. She wasn't knocking. She was slamming her face into his door, over and over, with the mechanical consistency of a failed boot sequence.
"Not a raid boss," Marcus whispered. "Definitely not a raid boss."
He backed away from the door on legs that felt borrowed from someone less cowardly.

Eight days. Eight days since the world had ended, and Marcus Chen—twenty-four years old, professionally unemployed, romantically invisible, biologically horizontal—had survived by doing what he did best: absolutely nothing. When the emergency broadcasts screamed FLEE, he'd locked his door. When his phone buzzed with evacuation alerts, he'd put it on silent. When his last Zoom interview had been interrupted by his interviewer being eaten on camera, he'd closed the laptop and opened his game.

The power had died on Day 3. His fridge had become a tomb of warm beer and questionable yogurt by Day 5. His pantry was down to two instant ramen cups, a sleeve of saltines, and one can of kidney beans he'd been saving for a scenario so desperate it could only be called "Tuesday."
He sat on his couch, staring at his dark monitor. The UPS battery had given out yesterday. No more games. No more internet forums. No more pretending the world outside wasn't happening.
The face-slapping at his door had stopped. Good. Maybe Mrs. Patterson had wandered off to ruin someone else's day.

Then, from the hallway: a different sound. Footsteps. Human-sounding footsteps, not the dragging shuffle of the dead. And crying. A woman crying, low and desperate, the kind of sound that couldn't be faked.
"Please," she whispered through the thin apartment walls. "Please, someone—"
A door slammed. A man's voice, sharp and cruel: "Shut up! You'll bring more of them!"
Marcus's hand twitched toward the door handle.
He pulled it back.

Since when did he help anyone? He was Marcus Chen, the guy who crossed the street to avoid Girl Scout cookie stands. The guy whose longest relationship was with his World of Warcraft guild. The guy whose own mother described him as "indoor-type" with the specific tone people used when they meant "disappointing."
The crying stopped. Either she'd given up, or—

A crash. Thedistinct sound of a door being kicked in. The woman screamed. The man yelled something unintelligible. Then wet sounds. The worst sounds Marcus had ever heard, and he'd once watched a YouTube video of someone opening a can of surströmming.
He locked his bedroom door too.
Night came like a held breath finally released.

Marcus lay on his bed—no sheets, he'd used them to seal the bathroom window—and listened to the city die. Gunshots in the distance, getting further apart. A helicopter that passed overhead once and never returned. The occasional car alarm, because even in the apocalypse, someone's Honda Civic needed to announce its distress.

His stomach cramped. The last ramen cup had been six hours ago, and ramen wasn't exactly known for its satiation curve. He thought about the kidney beans. He thought about how much he hated kidney beans. He thought about eating them anyway.
**[ROMANCE SYSTEM INITIALIZING...]**
Marcus sat up so fast his vision swam. Those words floated in the air in front of him—blocky, blue-white text like a game overlay, except this wasn't a game and he couldn't alt-tab out of it.

**[HOST DETECTED: MARCUS CHEN]**
**[COMPATIBILITY SCORE: 97.3%]**
**[REASON: "Chronically loveless, severely touch-starved, desperately lonely. Perfect candidate."]**
"I'm sorry, what?"

**[ROMANCE SYSTEM ACTIVE]**
**[Your survival now depends on your ability to form romantic connections.]**
**[Date. Flirt. Confess. Kiss.]**
**[Each romantic milestone unlocks survival rewards: food, shelter, defense, transport, and more.]**
**[Warning: Quests carry real physical risk. The System does not protect you from consequences.]**
**[First Quest Available]**

Marcus blinked. The text remained. He reached out to touch it—his fingers passed through like it was projected on his retinas. Which, given that the power was out and he hadn't slept in thirty-two hours, might mean he was having a breakdown.
But if he was hallucinating, his subconscious was a much better game designer than he'd ever been.

**[QUEST: A Stranger Outside]**
**[A female zombie has wandered to your hallway. She retains unusual physical preservation and faint consciousness signals.]**
**[Objective: Open your door and offer her food.]**
**[Risk Assessment: 73% chance she attacks on sight]**
**[Reward: 3x Fresh Bread Rolls, 1x Bottled Water, 1x System Strawberry]**
**[Bonus (if she accepts without attacking): Affection Meter Unlocked]**
**[Accept? Y/N]**
Through his peephole, Marcus could see her.

She stood in the hallway like a misplaced mannequin—a woman around his age, maybe younger, wearing what had once been an expensive charcoal business suit now torn at the shoulder. Her skin was pale as marble, eyes that strange zombie grey, but her features were sharp and symmetrical in a way that even the apocalypse couldn't ruin. High cheekbones. Full lips slightly parted. Dark hair that fell past her shoulders in a tangle that somehow looked deliberate.
She wasn't moving. Just standing there, head tilted, as if listening to something only she could hear.
Seventy-three percent chance she'd attack him. Which meant twenty-seven percent chance she wouldn't.
Marcus looked at his sealed door. At his empty pantry. At the kidney beans he was seriously considering.
"System," he said out loud, feeling ridiculous. "If I die, does it count as a bad ending?"

**[There are no save points. There are no respawns.]**
**[But there are rewards.]**
He thought about his empty stomach. About eight days of nothing. About the woman in the hallway who might have been someone's daughter, someone's colleague, someone's—
What did it matter? She was dead. They were all dead. He was going to be dead too, just slower.
Marcus Chen opened his door.

The zombie woman's head snapped toward him. Her grey eyes locked on his face with an intensity that made his stomach drop—not hunger, not quite, but something hungry-adjacent. Recognition, maybe. A faint flicker in those dead eyes, like static on a channel that almost came in.
He held out the sleeve of saltines. His hand was shaking so hard the crackers rattled in their plastic.
"Hey," he said, voice cracking. "You, uh... you hungry?"
She took a step toward him.
Then another.

**[AFFECTION METER UNLOCKED: SERAPHINA VALE]**
**[Current Affection: 3/100]**
**[Status: Curious]**
And for the first time in eight days, Marcus Chen felt something other than fear.
He felt like he'd just started a game he couldn't put down.
